title: How to boost a super nest egg
full_text:
Superannuation’s annual contribution caps climbed again this month, but most people do not realise just how much cash they can funnel into their retirement nest egg, and how fast they can do it.
Amid all the anger around Labor’s budget changes to negative gearing rules and capital gains tax, super skated through almost untouched, and its allure as a low-tax or no-tax savings structure is stronger than ever.
The only pain super savers have seen since May’s federal budget came a couple of weeks ago with Labor’s sudden move to ban investment property loans within self-managed super, as part of its controversial deal with the Greens.
However, for the vast majority of Australians saving for retirement, super is the lowest-tax way to grow a nest egg.
Its generous combination of contribution caps enables a generous sum of cash to be ploughed into super in just a five-year time frame.
How generous, you ask?
Almost $1.4m. And that’s per person, so it’s $2.8m for a couple, more than enough to deliver a very nice retirement indeed.
Here’s how the numbers work, including the latest contribution cap rises on July 1.
• Concessional contributions are tax-deductible contributions including compulsory employer super, salary sacrifice and voluntary personal contributions for which you claim a tax deduction. This cap climbed from $30,000 to $32,500 this month, so over five years your total injection could be $162,500.
• Non-concessional contributions are the lesser-known super sibling, because they are made with after-tax money – such as your cash in the bank or profits from an investment. Unlike concessional contributions that attract a 15 per cent contributions tax as your money enters your fund – still well below most people’s marginal tax rates – non-concessionals are tax-free, so every cent going into super gets a chance to grow. In a five-year period, that equates to $650,000 based on the current cap of $130,000, which rose from $120,000 on July 1.
• Even more non-concessional contributions can be squeezed into super using the “bring forward” rule, which allows people to bring forward the next two years of contributions. At the current cap, that’s another $260,000, although if Australia’s relatively high inflation rate continues, we could see the annual cap rise to $140,000 or $150,000 over the next five years.
• And if you decide to sell your family home and move elsewhere, consider downsizer contributions where up to $300,000 from the proceeds of selling a home can be injected into super above and beyond all the other caps. They are open to people over 55 with some conditions attached, and the one-off $300,000 cap is per person, not per couple.
That brings our five-year total super contribution potential to $1.37m, but there is still more.
Not to be confused with bring-forward non-concessional contributions, many people can also make carry-forward concessional contributions, which allows them to pump in unused cap amounts from up to five previous financial years, and claim the tax deduction they missed earlier.
There’s a key barrier – your super balance must be below $500,000 at the start of the financial year – but the potential super boost and tax deduction is impressive: up to $175,000 in 2026-27 for someone who hasn’t made any concessional contributions over the past five financial years.
MBA Financial Strategists director Darren James says many people do not realise just how much money they can plough into super.
“Most people have an understanding of the concessional cap per year, but they don’t understand how the carry-forward provision works and they normally don’t understand after-tax contributions and why you would do that,” he says.
“Most people are just focused on the immediate tax deduction, and not the longer term.” The biggest boost to retirement savings arguably comes not from those big, beautiful non-concessional contributions.
“If you know you don’t need to access that money, any earnings that that money is going to earn will be taxed at 15 per cent maximum as compared to your marginal tax rate,” James says.
“If you’re putting money away for what will be retirement, you can get the benefit of not paying tax on that money at your marginal tax rate.” JBS Financial Strategists chief executive Jenny Brown says the flexibility of superannuation contribution rules “often needs to be explained” to people.
“If you have a half-a-million-dollar windfall, you can get all of it in, and then some, in a short period of time,” Brown says.
The sheer size and flexibility of Australia’s combined super caps mean someone aged 60 can give their nest egg a massive boost by age 65 with any money they receive from inheritances or the sale of an investment property – something likely to rise in popularity as a result of Labor whacking future landlords with a big tax stick. As long as more super rule changes aren’t next in Labor’s firing line, harnessing the power of extra contributions will put millions more people on the path to a richer, tax-free retirement.
